original_reasoning: Arabic good name and Greek gracefulness/elegance can meet in the abstract field of good form and social propriety.
Arabic bad nourishment and Greek milk sit on opposite sides of the nourishment concept, so the semantic field is shared though polarity differs.
Arabic buds or lumps can conceptually point to protrusion, and a mountain peak preserves a raised protruding landform sense.

الصيم = الصلب الشديد المجتمع الخلق (hard, strong, compactly built). καταλύσιμος = dissolvable, destructible. These are genuine antonyms on the same axis: what is صيم (solid/unbreakable) is the opposite of καταλύσιμος (breakable). Both concepts presuppose the same structural-integrity framework. Antonymic cognates are a real phenomenon in Semitic-IE contact.

Arabic shakiyya = the remainder, residue. Greek stakte = oil of myrrh, distillate, what-drips-out (from stazo 'drip'). Both encode 'what is left after extraction'. Strong residue/distillate link. Phonetic resemblance shak/stak.
Plausible link through striking/throwing: Arabic gloss includes being hit or cast at; Greek is a spear-head.
دحا can involve spreading and taking the woman, which plausibly meets receive/take.
Wind striking with gravel/uncovering vs 'something poured/showered down' - shared pouring-down action
